title: Spatial Search by Nonlinear Quantum Walk
authors: David A. Meyer, Thomas G. Wong
abstract: Many-body quantum systems with effective nonlinearities have been shown to speed up quantum search on the complete graph, \textit{i.e.}, the combinatorial version of Grover's algorithm, at the expense of the number of particles needed for the effective nonlinearity to hold. Physically, however, data may not be arranged in an all-to-all network, and the task of searching incomplete graphs is the spatial search problem. We explore spatial search using a continuous-time nonlinear quantum walk on a variety of graphs. First, we consider incomplete graphs that are ``sufficiently complete'' so as to asymptotically search like the complete graph under a continuous-time (linear) quantum walk, which includes strongly regular graphs such as Paley graphs, regular graphs such as hypercubes, and irregular graphs such as complete bipartite graphs. For these sufficiently complete graphs, we analytically prove nonlinear speedups for Paley graphs and for complete bipartite graphs whose two partite sets both have size $Θ(N)$, for suitable cubic and cubic-quintic nonlinearities, and we give numerical evidence for stronger nonlinearities and for hypercubes. Second, we explore arbitrary-dimensional cubic lattices, and we numerically show that certain nonlinearities speed up search on sufficiently high dimensional lattices. Thus, nonlinear quantum search can remain viable even when the underlying graph is incomplete.
summary: This paper explores using nonlinear quantum walks to perform spatial search on incomplete graphs, moving beyond the idealized all-to-all connectivity of the complete graph. By analyzing the dynamics using nonlinear Schrödinger equations, the authors prove that specific nonlinearities can maintain the quantum speedup on graphs like Paley graphs and complete bipartite graphs. This demonstrates that nonlinear quantum search remains viable even when the underlying physical network is sparse.
main_problem: The paper investigates how nonlinear quantum walks can be used to achieve quantum search on graphs that are not fully connected (incomplete graphs), extending the concept beyond the standard complete graph search.
main_result: Nonlinear quantum walks can successfully speed up the search process on specific incomplete graphs, such as Paley graphs and complete bipartite graphs, provided the nonlinearity coefficients are appropriately chosen.
method: The authors employ theoretical analysis using nonlinear Schrödinger equations, comparing the nonlinear evolution to the known linear evolution via error bounding techniques like Duhamel's formula.
model_or_system: The model is a continuous-time quantum walk governed by a nonlinear Hamiltonian, which includes a nonlinear self-potential term dependent on the probability density. The analysis is applied to various graph structures, including complete graphs, Paley graphs, hypercubes, and cubic lattices.
key_observables: Success Probability (probability of finding the particle at the marked vertex), Energy Gap ($\Delta E$), and the required search time $T_N$.
important_parameters: Nonlinearity coefficients ($g$), graph size ($N$), and the lattice dimension ($d$). The analysis often requires conditions like $g \ll \sqrt{N}$ or $g=N-1$.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the graphs being 'sufficiently complete' to mimic the behavior of a complete graph, and the mathematical proofs depend on bounding the accumulated error term ($\eta(t)$) to be negligible.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the success probability vs. time for linear and nonlinear searches on various graphs (Complete, Paley, Hypercube, Bipartite) across different dimensions, showing the maintenance of high success probability under nonlinear control.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the linear search baseline on complete graphs, extending the concept to 'sufficiently complete' graphs, and then rigorously analyzing the nonlinear dynamics using error bounding techniques to prove speedups for specific nonlinearities (cubic, cubic-quintic) on these graphs.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses the practical limitations of quantum search algorithms by showing that nonlinear interactions can extend the speedup capability to real-world, incomplete network structures, which is a key area of research in quantum information.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) A complete graph of N = 6 vertices. Subfigures (b) through (d) are sufficiently complete graphs, with (b) a Paley graph of N = 9 vertices, (c) a 4-dimensional hypercube with 24 = 16 vertices, and (d) a complete bipartite graph with N1 = 3 and N2 = 4 vertices in each partite set. In all, a vertex is marked by an oracle, indicated by a double circle. Identically evolving vertices are identically colored and numerically labeled.

FIG. 2. Success probability as a function of time for quantum search on various graphs with various nonlinearities (NLs) using γc(t) (13) with ℓ= 1. The first row [subfigures (a)-(d)] is the linear (g = 0) algorithm, the second row [subfigures (e)-(h)] is the cubic nonlinearity f(p) = p with g = N −1, the third row [subfigures (i)-(l)] is the cubic-quintic nonlinearity f(p) = p −p2

FIG. 3. Success probability as a function of time for quantum search on various graphs with various nonlinearities using γc(t) (13) with ℓchosen to be the vertices adjacent to the marked vertex. The first row [subfigures (a)-(d)] is the linear (g = 0) algorithm, the second row [subfigures (e)-(h)] is the cubic nonlinearity f(p) = p with g = N −1, the third row [subfigures (i)-(l)] is the cubic-quintic nonlinearity f(p) = p −p2 with g = N −1, and the last row [subfigures (m)-(p)] is the loglinear nonlinearity f(p) = ln p with g = √
